segments.s426-g121: پراگنده گشتند is a compound verbal phrase: پراگنده (an adjective 'scattered') comes before the plural past verb گشتند ('they became/they were' — here rendered as an intransitive perfect meaning 'were scattered'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and آورِدگاه (آوردگاه) is the place noun 'battlefield'; the whole chunk means 'they were scattered from the battlefield.' Word order (adjective + گشتند) is typical in Persian for a state that came about.
segments.s426-g122: This is a verbless/descriptive clause where غمی گشته is a participial phrase 'having become grief-stricken' (غمی = 'grief', گشته = 'become/turned') that attaches to the coordinated noun اسپان و مردان ('horses and men'). تباه is a postpositive adjective meaning 'ruined' and applies to the men (and by context to the group). The sequence shows poetic economy: the participle precedes the nouns it modifies.
segments.s426-g123: کف اندر دهانشان شده is a fixed descriptive clause: کف here means 'foam' (kaf) and اندر = 'in/within', دهانشان = 'their mouth(s)', شده = 'has become' (perfect/participial). خون و خاک ('blood and dust') are the substances that have become the foam in their mouths — so the idiom means 'their mouths foamed with blood and dust' or 'their mouths were full of blood and dust.'
segments.s426-g124: The phrase is a nominal predicate: همه ('all/everyone') + گبر ('gabr', here 'the infidels/pagans') joined by و to برگستوان (an archaic/compound noun) and finished by the intensive resultative adjective چاک‌چاک meaning 'torn/rent to pieces.' Grammatically, چاک‌چاک is a predicate adjective describing the preceding coordinated nouns; the reduplicated form emphasizes the completeness of the tearing.
segments.s426-g41: بفرمود تا is a polite/honorific verb + subordinating تا introducing a commanded action: 'he ordered that...'. جوشن is the direct object 'the cuirass'. و خود اوی is an emphatic possessive phrase: خود (emphatic 'self/own') plus the enclitic اوی ('him/he') yielding 'his own' or 'himself' used here to mean 'his own [equipment]'.
segments.s426-g42: همان here points to a specific item just mentioned ('that' / 'the same'); ترک + ش is the split form of ترکش (quiver) where ش is the possessive clitic 'his'. و links the following noun (نیزه 'spear'). جنگجوی is a descriptive/attributive noun 'warrior' modifying نیزه ('the warrior's spear').
segments.s426-g43: ببردند is a past plural verb 'they took/carried' (perfective). و links the clause to the next action. پوشید here is 3rd singular past 'he put on / wore'. جوشن is the object 'the cuirass'. برش is a contracted prepositional/clitic combination meaning 'on him' (here written as one token); its dictionary senses given do not include this clitic meaning, so the appropriate sense index is -1.
segments.s426-g44: نهاد is the past of نهادن 'to place' ('he/they placed'). آن کلاه کیی is a noun phrase 'that Kayanian helmet' (کیی = Kayanian, an attributive proper/adjectival marker). بر سرش is the prepositional phrase 'upon his head' (بر = on, سر = head, ش = his).
segments.s426-g45: بفرمود تا again introduces an order ('he commanded that...'). زین بر اسپ سیاه is the noun phrase 'the saddle on the black steed' (زین = saddle; بر = on; اسپ = steed/horse; سیاه = black modifying اسپ).
segments.s426-g46: نهادند is past plural 'they placed/put'. و links the two verbs. بردند is past plural 'they carried/brought'. نزدیک شاه is an adverbial phrase 'near the king' (نزدیک = near; شاه = king). Together: 'they placed it and brought it near the king.'
segments.s426-g47: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. جوشن بپوشید is a verb phrase with جوشن (cuirass) as object and بپوشید as 3rd‑person past 'he put on / donned'. پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'aggressive/combative' serving as the resulting state or predicate (he 'became' aggressive).
segments.s426-g48: ز ... و ز ... is a repeated preposition 'from/ because of' introducing two causes: زور 'strength' and شادی 'joy'. که بود is a relative clause marker + past copula 'that was' and اندر اوی means 'in him' (اندر = in, اوی = him/he). The whole clause gives the reason for his aggressiveness: 'because of the strength and joy that were in him.'
segments.s426-g57: پس از here forms a temporal phrase 'after (a while)'; پس carries the sense 'after' and از is the preposition 'from/of' used in the fixed sequence پس از. با + name (با پشوتن) = 'with Peshotan.' بگفت is an archaic/poetic past third-person form (ب‑ prefix + گفت) meaning 'he said/spoke.'
segments.s426-g58: که introduces reported speech ('that...'). ما را uses را as the definite/object marker marking 'us' as the logical object. نباید is an impersonal/modal negative ('must not/should not') and here governs an implied copular construction. بدو = 'to him' (dative), and یار و جفت are coordinate nouns 'companion and mate' (جفت here = 'mate'); the verb 'to be' is omitted after نباید in this idiomatic construction ('we must not be to him a companion/mate').
segments.s426-g59: چو is the conditional/temporal conjunction ('if' / 'when') here used as 'if.' تنهاست is a present/state predicate 'is alone' (third person). The main clause follows: ما نیز تنها شویم — ما 'we', نیز 'also', and شویم is a present-subjunctive/optative first-person-plural ('let us become' / 'we should become') meaning 'we should be/become alone' in response to the condition.
segments.s426-g60: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; پستی here means 'lowness/baseness' (the low state or humble position). بران ('thereon' / 'from that') points back to the mentioned low state. تند is an adverb 'swift(ly)' modifying the verbal phrase بالا شویم, where بالا شویم ('we rise up' / 'we ascend') uses شویم again in an optative/intentional sense — 'we will/should rise quickly from that low condition.'
segments.s426-g61: بران گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in that manner' or 'thus.' رفتند is the simple past third-person plural 'they went.' هر دو 'both' quantifies the subject and appears after the verb (flexible word order common in poetry). به رزم is a prepositional phrase 'to battle' indicating destination/purpose.
segments.s426-g62: تو گفتی is the reporting clause 'you said' (گفتی is the past second-person form 'you said' used to introduce speech). که introduces the reported content: اندر جهان نیست بزم — اندر + جهان = 'in the world,' نیست = 'is not/there is not,' and بزم ('banquet/feast; revelry/assembly') is the predicate noun; the clause states 'there is no revelry in the world.' Note that the provided sense-list for گفتی does not match the verb sense, so that item is marked accordingly.
segments.s426-g63: چو is the literary conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. نزدیک گشتند is a compound expression: نزدیک (near/approaching) + گشتند (past plural of گشتن/گشت#گرد 'become/turn'), here meaning 'they became near' or 'they approached' (so the whole verb phrase = 'drew near'). پیر و جوان are coordinated nouns 'the old and the young' (subject), placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s426-g64: دو is the numeral 'two' modifying شیر (lion); شیر here is used metaphorically for brave men ('lions'), not 'milk'. سرافراز is a postpositive adjective 'proud/triumphant' describing the lions. و is the coordinating 'and', and دو پهلوان repeats the numeral + noun pattern: 'two champions' (پهلوان = hero/champion).
segments.s426-g73: بگو is an imperative 'say/tell'. تا here expresses purpose ('so that / in order that') and introduces a clause of intention. سوار آورم is verb + object with آورم as 1st‑person present/subjunctive/optative meaning 'I may bring / I will bring' (classical style); زابلی is an adjectival/relational word after the noun, ‘a rider from Zabul’. The whole segment = 'Tell (me) so that I may bring a Zabli rider.'
segments.s426-g74: که is the relative conjunction 'who/that' introducing a relative clause modifying the rider. باشند is 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive/optative of 'to be' (here meaning 'who will be' or 'who may be'); با + noun marks accompaniment or instrument ('with' / 'armed with'). خنجر کابلی is a noun phrase 'a Kabul dagger' (کابلی modifying خنجر). The clause = 'who will be with / armed with a Kabul dagger.'
segments.s426-g75: برین is the fused poetic/colloquial form of بر + این (here meaning 'on/at that' or 'to that'); رزمگه‌شان is 'their battlefield' (رزمگه + possessive شان). به جنگ آوریم places the purpose 'to/for battle' before آوریم: آوریم is 1st‑person (optative/habitual) 'I/we will bring' (classical usage often uses plural for speaker), so the phrase means 'I will bring (them) to that battlefield for battle.'
segments.s426-g76: خود is emphatic 'myself / personally'. ایدر is the adverb 'here'. زمانی here means 'for a while' (temporal duration). درنگ آوریم combines the noun/infinitive درنگ 'a pause / delay' with آوریم ('I will cause/bring about'), a common classical formula meaning 'I will wait / pause.' Altogether: 'I myself will pause here for a while.'
segments.s426-g77: بباشد is a 3rd‑person jussive/optative 'may it be'. به کام تو is an idiom 'to your wish / to your liking' (preposition + noun + pronoun). خون ریختن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'the shedding of blood' (ریختن used here in the literal sense 'to spill/shed'). The clause expresses a wish: 'May the shedding of blood be to your liking.'
segments.s426-g78: ببینی is 2nd‑person present/jussive 'may you see' (an optative wish directed at 'you'). تگاپوی is a proper name (object of ببینی). و joins the next nominal phrase; آویختن is the infinitive/gerund 'hanging' (to hang / the act of hanging). Grammatically the two items are objects of 'see' (i.e., 'may you see Tegapuy and hanging').
segments.s426-g79: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' qualifying the manner of the reply. پاسخ is the noun 'answer/reply'. آوردش is past tense 'brought/produced it' with the enclitic -ش ('to him' or 'it'), used idiomatically here as 'he answered' or 'he gave such a reply'. اسفندیار is the proper name (the subject), placed after the verb in poetic order.
segments.s426-g80: که introduces the reported speech or the content of the answer ('that' / 'namely'). چندین is an adverb 'many times' / 'repeatedly' modifying the speech-verb. چه گویی is an interrogative phrase: چه 'what' (or exclamatory 'why/how') + گویی (here the present-form 'you say' — an archaic/poetic verbal form), so together 'what do you say' / 'why do you say'. چنین before نابکار means 'such' and نابکار 'wicked one' is an accusatory adjective/noun addressing the addressee. (Note: the listed senses for گویی in the prompt do not include the verbal sense 'you say', so the sense index for گویی is set to -1.)
segments.s426-g33: گذشت is a simple past verb ('passed'); the prepositional phrase از لب رود ('from/out of the river's lip/edge') follows it as its complement. بالا گرفت is a two-word verbal phrase where بالا is an adverb 'up/aloft' and گرفت (past of گرفتن) functions with it to mean 'rose/took up (height)'. The coordinating و simply links the two past actions.
segments.s426-g34: همی ماند is a past narrative construction with همی giving a durative/continuous nuance ('still/continued to') and ماند (past of ماندن) meaning 'remained/was left'. از introduces the cause/agent here ('by/at' the following phrase), کار گیتی is a noun phrase 'the deed/affair of the world,' and شگفت is used as a predicative notion of wonder — the whole clause means the world remained astonished at that deed.
segments.s426-g35: خروشید is a past verb/narration meaning 'a roar sounded/it roared' (impersonal or third-person action). کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing someone, followed by فرخ اسفندیار ('fortunate Esfandiyar') as the addressee. The word order is vocative after the verb of sound, typical in epic narration.
segments.s426-g36: هماوردت is the noun هماورد with the second-person possessive/accusative clitic -ت ('your rival/opponent'). آمد is the past 'came'. برآرای here functions as a verbal/adverbal notion 'to arrange/prepare for' (classical usage meaning 'for the purpose of setting in order'), and کار is 'the affair/deed' (here contextually 'the fight' or 'the action'). The phrase means that the addressee's opponent has arrived to set things for the coming action.
segments.s426-g37: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. بشنید is a past-tense verb 'heard' (third-person past). The proper name اسفندیار is the subject, and این سخن ('this/these words') is the direct object; the clause is a standard temporal clause 'when Esfandiyar heard these words'.
segments.s426-g38: ازان is a classical/formal contraction meaning 'from that' or 'of that' and introduces the source of the speech. شیر is figurative 'lion' (a kenning for a brave warrior), پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'aggressive/quarrelsome', and کهن is 'old/ancient' — together 'that ancient quarrelsome lion'. Word order is typical Persian noun phrase with modifiers following the head.
segments.s426-g39: بخندید is a past-tense verb 'he laughed'. The conjunction و links it to گفت 'he said'. اینک is an adverb 'now' or 'behold', and آراستم is a past-perfect-styled first-person form 'I have adorned/dressed/armed myself' (classical reflexive sense 'I am equipped/ready'). The clause reports speech and the speaker's self-statement of readiness.
segments.s426-g40: بدانگه is an adverbial phrase 'at that moment/thereupon'. که introduces a subordinate clause explaining the circumstance, and از خواب برخاستم is a first-person past 'I rose from sleep' (از خواب = 'from sleep', برخاستم = 'I rose'). Together it qualifies the preceding statement: he had just risen from sleep.
segments.s426-g81: ز ایوان به شبگیر برخاستی is a prepositional phrase followed by the past tense verb برخاستی (2nd person singular). ز (poetic form of از) means “from”; ایوان is the place “iwan/porch”; به + شبگیر gives a temporal/directional sense “at/to nightfall” (شبگیر functioning as a temporal adjective/noun); برخاستی is the simple past ‘you rose/stand up’. Word order is prepositional phrases before the verb, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s426-g82: ازین تند بالا مرا خواستی: ازین = از + این, “from this”; تند بالا is a two-word descriptive phrase for the place/height (تند here roughly ‘swift/steep’ and بالا ‘height/high’); مرا is the object (م + را split orthographically in the data: م = part of ‘me’, را = object marker); خواستی is the past 2nd person singular of خواستن, here ‘you sought/asked for me’. The whole phrase means ‘from this steep/high place you sought me’.
segments.s426-g83: چرا ساختی بند و مکر و فریب begins with the interrogative چرا “why”. ساختی is past 2nd-person singular ‘you made/devised’. Then three coordinated nouns (بند ‘bond/snare’, مکر ‘deceit’, فریب ‘deception’) are objects/accusations. The verb governs the coordinated nouns; this is a direct rhetorical question accusing the addressee of contriving traps and trickery.
segments.s426-g84: همانا بدیدی به تنگی نشیب: همانا is an emphatic adverb ‘truly/verily’. بدیدی is past 2nd-person singular of دیدن ‘you saw’. به introduces the following noun phrase as direction/condition (‘into/at’), تنگی ‘narrowness’ describes the quality, and نشیب ‘descent/decline’ is the noun being described. Together: ‘you truly saw (it) — the narrow descent’ (i.e., you perceived the tight/steep pass).
segments.s426-g85: چه باید مرا جنگ زابلستان is a rhetorical construction: چه ‘what’ introduces the question, باید is the modal ‘must/ought/need’, and مرا (m + را split in the data) marks the experiencer/object ‘for me’. جنگ زابلستان is a nominal phrase ‘war of Zabulistan’. The sense is: ‘What need is there for me of a war with Zabulistan?’ — the verb ‘to be needed’ is expressed by باید plus the indirect object مرا and the nominal phrase.
segments.s426-g86: وگر جنگ ایران و کابلستان: وگر ‘or if/but if’ introduces an alternative condition; جنگ ایران و کابلستان is a parallel nominal phrase ‘the war of Iran and Kabulistan’. There is no new verb; this clause continues the previous rhetorical question, offering an alternative: ‘or if (there is) a war, (should it be) Iran and Kabulistan?’
segments.s426-g87: مبادا چنین هرگز آیین من is an emphatic negative wish/exclamation: مبادا ‘may it not be / lest’, چنین ‘such/so’, هرگز ‘ever/never’ and آیین من ‘my custom/way’. The construction places the emphatic negation before the predicate, yielding ‘May such a thing never be my custom.’
segments.s426-g88: سزا نیست این کار در دین من is a statement of propriety: سزا نیست ‘it is not fitting/appropriate’ (سزا here in the sense of ‘desert/fitness’), این کار ‘this deed’, and در دین من ‘in my religion/way’. The predicate سزا نیست precedes the topic ‘این کار’ with the locative phrase specifying the moral/religious domain.
segments.s426-g113: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. شیران (plural) is the noun 'lions' used metaphorically for fierce warriors; جنگی is an adjectival modifier 'warlike/fighting' placed after the noun in this poetic order. برآشوفتند is a past-tense, 3rd-person-plural verb (بر + آشفتن) meaning 'they rushed upon' or 'they sprang at' — the prefix بر- intensifies the action.
segments.s426-g114: پر از خشم is a prepositional phrase 'full of anger' (پر + از + noun) modifying اندامها. اندامها ('the bodies/limbs') is the plural noun serving as topic/subject. کوفتند is the past 3rd-person-plural verb 'they struck/beat' — the object is implicit (they struck at one another or struck with their limbs).
segments.s426-g115: همان دسته is a demonstrative nominal phrase 'that very group / the same band.' بشکست is a past-tense verb 'broke' (3rd-person singular here agreeing with دسته) used transitively/intransitively in poetic syntax. گرز گران is the noun phrase 'heavy maces' (گرز = mace, گران = heavy/weighty) placed after the verb in this condensed poetic word order.
segments.s426-g116: فروماند is a past-state verb meaning 'were worn out / became exhausted.' از کار is a prepositional phrase 'from work / from exertion.' دست سران is a genitive construction 'the hands of the chiefs' (دست = hand/power, سران = chiefs/heads). Together the phrase says the chiefs' hands were spent from toil.
segments.s426-g117: گرفتند is a past 3rd-person-plural verb 'they seized/took.' زان پس is a fused adverbial expression 'thereupon / after that' (زان = 'from that', پس = 'then/after'). دوال is an archaic/poetic numeral/adjective 'pair/twofold' modifying کمر, here 'girth(s) / belt(s)' (کمر used as 'girth' or 'girdle' in a riding context).
segments.s426-g118: دو اسپ = 'two steeds/ horses' (here represented by اسپ). تگاور is a noun 'rider/warrior' that functions adjectivally here (the riders of those steeds). فروبرده is a perfect/past-participle form 'having thrust down / having plunged,' and سر is its object 'head.' The whole unit describes the two horses whose riders had thrust down their heads.
segments.s426-g119: همی زور کرد is a continuous or iterative past expression 'they kept exerting strength / they strove' (همی marking ongoing action, زور = force/strength, کرد = did/exerted). این بران آن برین is a paired adverbial distribution: این بران = 'this one on that (near) side' and آن برین = 'that one on this (other) side'; بران/برین are formed with بر ('on/upon') + a demonstrative, the final ین here being an orthographic/poetic variant of این.
segments.s426-g120: نجنبید is the negative past 'did not move / did not stir' (3rd-person singular/plural depending on context). یک شیر is the subject 'a single lion' (here likely a metaphor for one unshaken rider). بر پشت زین is a locative phrase 'on the back of the saddle' (بر = on, پشت = back, زین = saddle). The verb precedes the subject in this poetic inversion.
segments.s426-g105: نخستین is an adverbial/temporal modifier 'first'. به نیزه is a prepositional phrase 'toward/onto the spear(s)'. برآویختند is a past tense, 3rd-person plural verb (compound br- + āvikhtan) meaning 'they sprang upon' or 'they leapt at' — subject is omitted as usual in Persian.
segments.s426-g106: همی is a classical progressive/continuous marker (here indicating repeated/ongoing action). خون is the noun 'blood'. ز جوشن is a prepositional phrase 'from the cuirass/armor'. فرو + ریختند is a verbal compound: فرو (down-) + ریختند (past of ریختن 'to pour/fall') giving 'poured down' — verb final, 3rd-person plural.
segments.s426-g107: چنین introduces the resultive description 'thus/so'. تا here introduces a result clause ('so that' / 'such that'). سنانها (pl.) is 'the spear-points'. به هم is a reciprocal phrase 'against one another' (به = toward/onto, هم = together/with). برشکست is a past 3rd-plural verb 'broke/shattered' (poetic form).
segments.s426-g108: به شمشیر here marks the instrument/means 'by/with the sword'. بردند is the past 3rd-plural of بردن 'to take/take up' (used in the idiom دست بردن) meaning 'they took up' or 'they drew'. ناچار is an adverb/adjective 'compelled/forced' modifying the action. دست ('hand') appears after the verb in poetic inversion as part of the light-verb construction دست بردن ('to lay hands on' = 'to draw/use').
segments.s426-g109: به آوردگه is a locative prepositional phrase 'to/at the place of meeting/encounter'. گردن is the noun 'neck' and here forms the idiom گردن افراختن. افراختند is past 3rd-plural 'they raised/erected' — together meaning 'they raised/bared their necks' (a poetic expression of presenting themselves for combat).
segments.s426-g110: چپ و راست is a coordinated phrase 'left and right' (i.e. both flanks). هر دو 'both' emphasizes that both sides acted. همی is a classical aspectual particle indicating continued/characteristic action. تاختند is past 3rd-plural 'they charged/attacked' (تاختن in the sense 'to rush/attack').
segments.s426-g111: ز (poetic preposition) means 'from/by'. نیروی اسپان is a genitive construction 'the force/strength of the horses' (نیروی = strength/force, اسپان = horses). و connects the phrases. زخم سران is 'wounds/strikes to the heads' or 'wounds of the leaders/heads' (زخم = wound/blow, سران = heads/ leaders) — both phrases give causes.
segments.s426-g112: شکسته شد is a passive/perfect construction 'were broken' (شکسته = broken, شد used as auxiliary/passive). آن تیغهای گران is the (postverbal) nominal phrase 'those heavy/precious swords' (آن = the/those, تیغهای = swords, گران = costly/heavy), placed after the verb in poetic inversion.
segments.s426-g65: خروش آمد is a simple past/clausal construction meaning “a roar arose.” از باره (from/of the matter) = “concerning” or “because of,” where از + باره (= این باره) frames the cause. هر دو مرد places quantity after the prepositional phrase (literally “each/both two men”) and here مرد has the poetic sense “hero/valiant man.”
segments.s426-g66: تو گفتی is an address + past verb “you said.” بدرید here is an imperative/optative sense meaning “let them rend/tear” (classical poetic subjunctive/imperative for a plural third person). دشت نبرد is a compound noun phrase “the plain/field of battle” (دشت = plain, نبرد = battle) and is the direct object of the quoted command.
segments.s426-g67: چنین گفت رستم is a typical narrative order (“thus spoke Rostam”), with گفت as the past-tense reporting verb and رستم the subject. به آواز سخت uses به + noun to indicate manner (“in a voice”) and سخت functions adverbially “strongly/harshly,” i.e. “in a strong voice.”
segments.s426-g68: که‌ای is the poetic vocative particle (equivalent to که + ای) used to address someone directly: “O …”. شاه is the addressee “king,” and the two epithets شادان‌دل and نیک‌بخت are adjectival compounds (“cheerful/joyful‑hearted” and “fortunate/well‑fated”) joined by و.
segments.s426-g69: ازین گونه = from/′in this kind/way′ (از + این + گونه) sets the manner. مستیز is an archaic adjective/participial form meaning “be drunk/intoxicated,” and بد is “bad/wicked.” را here is the object/topic marker attached to the adjectives, and مکوش is a negative imperative “do not be / do not do” (مَکوش, lit. “do not try/be”), so the clause forbids behaving in that way.
segments.s426-g70: سوی مردمی is a prepositional phrase “toward the people” (سوی = toward, مردمی = the people/folk). یاز is an archaic imperative “open” (imperative of باز کردن / یازیدن in older usage). بازآر is another imperative “bring back/restore,” and هوش is the object “wit/sense/awareness” — together: “turn to the people, open (your) mind, and restore your sense.”
segments.s426-g71: اگر introduces a conditional clause “if.” جنگ خواهی is “you desire/want war” (خواهی = present/future‑oriented second‑person form of خواستن). و خون ریختن links another object: خون ریختن is a verbal noun + infinitive “the shedding/spilling of blood” (i.e. bloodshed).
segments.s426-g72: برین گونه = بر + این + گونه “upon/over in this manner”; سختی is a noun “hardship/severity.” برآویختن is an infinitive meaning “to rise up/attack” or more generally “to bring about/raise” and here functions as the result/ consequence of the conditional (i.e. such severe hardship will be raised/visited upon [you/people]).
segments.s426-g17: بیامد is a past verb 'he came' (verb before subject omitted, a common poetic order). چنان is an adverb 'so/thus' modifying the manner/extent, and تا here means 'as far as' marking the limit: 'he came so as far as'. لب هیرمند is a prepositional/locative phrase 'the bank/brink of the Helmand' (لب = 'brink', followed by the river name).
segments.s426-g18: This is a verbless descriptive clause: همه دل پر از باد = 'every/ all heart full of wind' (پر 'full (of)' takes از here), using باد literally 'wind' but idiomatically for agitation/ardor; و links the second coordinated phrase لب پر ز پند = 'and lip(s) full of advice' where ز is the poetic variant of از. Word order is typical nominal-description in poetry (subject + predicate adjective).
segments.s426-g19: سپه is the subject 'the army/host'. با برادر 'with (his) brother' is an accompanying prepositional phrase; هم آنجا بماند = 'also remained there' with بماند (present/past poetic form 'remained') placed after the locative آنجا. The particle هم marks 'also/together' and links this action to what precedes.
segments.s426-g20: سوی introduces direction 'toward(s)'. لشکر شاه ایران is a genitive chain 'the army of the king of Iran'. براند is a poetic/archaic verb form meaning 'drove/sent/moved (toward)' (third person form with the final -د); the subject is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s426-g21: چنین گفت پس = 'then he said thus' (چنین 'thus', گفت here is the past-speech verb 'said' in poetical narration). با زواره به راز = 'to Zovareh in secret' where با + name marks the addressee and به راز 'in private/secret' qualifies the manner of speaking.
segments.s426-g22: که is the complementizer introducing reported content 'that'. مردیست is the copular construction 'is a man / he is' (مردی‌ست = 'he is a man/ a man is'), followed by the demonstrative این 'this' and the epithets بدرگ دیوساز describing the person: بدرگ an epithet meaning wicked/ill (here used pejoratively), and دیوساز a compound 'demon‑maker' (i.e. sorcerer/troublemaker).
segments.s426-g23: بترسم is first‑person 'I fear' (present tense used to introduce a clause of apprehension). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that'. با او = 'with him/against him'. نیارم is an archaic/poetic negative-potential form meaning roughly 'I may not be able' or 'I shall not succeed' (the supplied lemma senses do not match this usage, so the sense index is set to -1); زدن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'to strike' and is the action feared.
segments.s426-g24: ندانم = 'I do not know' (main clause). کزین پس 'from this on / henceforth' specifies the time frame. چه 'what' is an interrogative/exclamative, شاید is a modal 'it may be / perhaps' and بدن 'the body' (here 'what may become of the body' is intended), so the clause literally asks 'what may be to the body from now on.'
segments.s426-g9: بیامد is a past 3rd‑person verb 'he came' placed before its locative zواره (verb–place order is common in epic Persian). سپه گرد کرد is a compound where گرد (here verbal/'gather/assemble') combines with the light verb کرد 'made/did' to mean 'marshaled/assembled the army.' گرد functions as the verb-stem and کرد is the light verb.
segments.s426-g10: به میدان کار و به دشت نبرد are destination phrases introduced by the preposition به 'into/towards.' میدان کار is a compound 'field (mīdān) of action (kār)' and دشت نبرد 'plain of battle' is parallel; both specify where the army was sent.
segments.s426-g11: همی is the archaic/progressive particle indicating continued or habitual action; همی رفت thus means 'kept going' or 'advanced.' The phrase نیزه به دست is an instrumental/adjunct ('spear in hand' — به here indicates the instrument/possession), placed after the verb for vivid description.
segments.s426-g12: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; بیرون شد is a small clause meaning 'stepped/went out' (شد here is the past-form auxiliary/verb used with an adverbial), and از جایگاه نشست is the result/action 'he sat down' with از جایگاه ('out of/from his place') indicating where he emerged from.
segments.s426-g13: سپاهش is the noun سپاه 'army' with the possessive suffix -ش 'his' (i.e. 'his army'). خواندند is a 3rd‑person plural past 'they called/cried out,' and آفرین is the shouted acclamation 'Bravo!' برو here is an imperative/exclamation within that shout ('go on'/'onward'). Word order places the possessor-suffixed noun before the verb, then the shouted word.
segments.s426-g14: که introduces the wish clause; بی‌تو 'without you' is the complement, and مباد is the negative optative 'may it not be' (a conventional classical wish formula). The items اسپ و گوپال و زین are the objects of the wish ('horse/steed, Gopāl (proper name), and saddle'), listed in parallel.
segments.s426-g15: همی رفت again shows ongoing/progressive action 'kept going/advanced.' The proper name رستم follows the verb (typical epic word order), and زواره پسش is an adverbial phrase indicating direction/place 'to Zowārah' with پسش 'after him/behind him' attached to show sequence or following.
segments.s426-g16: کجا is the interrogative 'where' (here rhetorical), بود is the past tense 'was,' and در پادشاهی کسش is the locative phrase 'in his kingship/kingdom' with کسش ('someone/anyone, with possessive -ش')—the clause asks rhetorically who remained in the rulership after him.
segments.s426-g97: توی here addresses the other party before the predicate (poetic/vulgar vocative use of 'you are' — not listed among the provided senses). جنگجوی is the predicate noun ‘warrior’. و coordinates two parallel predications; منم combines the pronoun من with the enclitic copula -م meaning 'I am', and جنگخواه is a noun/epithet meaning 'one who desires war' (a battle-seeker). The overall structure is two balanced predicative phrases: 'You are X and I am Y.'
segments.s426-g98: بگردیم is 1st-person-plural optative/subjunctive 'let us go/turn/engage' (poetic shading); یک با دگر is an idiomatic phrase 'one with the other' = 'each against the other'; بی‌سپاه is an adverbial/adjunct 'without an army' qualifying how they will meet. Word order places the verb بگردیم before the coordinated phrase that specifies the participants and the circumstance.
segments.s426-g99: ببینیم is 1st-person-plural optative/subjunctive 'let us see' (introducing an inquiry). تا here functions as a subordinate-marker 'until/so that / to see whether' leading into the clause that follows. اسپ اسفندیار is a possessive/nominal group 'Esfandiyar's steed' (noun + proper name in apposition).
segments.s426-g100: سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)'; آخور is the goal noun 'manger/stable'; آید is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive 'comes' (classical/poetic present); همی is an archaic emphatic/continuous verbal particle (here adding emphasis or habitual/ongoing sense to the verb); بی‌سوار is a predicative adjective 'riderless' describing the steed. Order is preposition + goal + verb + emphatic particle + predicate.
segments.s426-g101: وگر is the conditional conjunction 'if' (often introducing a counterfactual/alternate case); باره here means 'occasion/instance' (sense 'again' or 'on another occasion' in this context); رستم جنگجوی is the name with an epithet 'Rostam, the warrior' (noun + appositive adjective). The cluster sets up an alternative conditional subject: 'if again Rostam the warrior...'.
segments.s426-g102: به is the directional preposition 'toward/into' and ایوان the place 'iwan/porch' (goal). نهد is the classical 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of نهاد 'to place/put' used here as a verb meaning 'sets/places' or more broadly 'appears/puts himself'; بی‌خداوند is a compound with the negative prefix بی- 'without' + خداوند 'lord' meaning 'without a lord' or 'lordless/unaccompanied by his lord'; روی is 'face' used idiomatically to mean 'appearance/manifestation'. Together the phrase describes Rostam's coming/appearance at the iwan lacking the lord's accompaniment or favor.
segments.s426-g103: نهادند is a past-tense plural 'they set/established' (here 'they made'); پیمان is the direct object 'pact/covenant'; دو جنگی is a numeral + noun phrase 'two fighters' (i.e. the two combatants party to the pact); که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/so that'; کس in the upcoming negative clause means 'no one' (used with the negative verb to express absence of any person).
segments.s426-g104: نباشد is the negative subjunctive/optative construction meaning 'there be not' / 'shall not be' (classical negative used to express prohibition or stipulation). بران is a fused prepositional/adverbial 'on/for that' (= بر + آن) pointing back to the mentioned battle; جنگ is 'battle/war'; فریادرس is a noun 'rescuer' or 'one who comes to aid'. Together the clause states the pact's condition: 'that no one be a rescuer in that battle.'
segments.s426-g1: چو شد روز = a temporal clause 'when the day became/when day came' where چو (conjunction) + شد (past of شدن 'become') set the time. رستم (subject) + بپوشید (past 3rd sg. of پوشیدن 'to put on') then the object گبر (the costume/identity he put on). The verb precedes its object here, a normal Persian SOV variant in poetry.
segments.s426-g2: نگهبان تن کرد is a light‑verb construction: تن کردن ('to take on a body/guise') = 'to assume (a role/guise)', so نگهبان (guard) is the role assumed and کرد is the past auxiliary 'he made/did'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' introducing what follows; گبر again names the garb/identity. (The final ببر appears in the manuscript line as a short word; here the core grammatical unit is تن کرد with نگهبان as complement.)
segments.s426-g3: کمندی (indefinite noun) = 'a lasso/loop'. به + فتراک زین is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto the saddle‑cloth (فتراک) of the saddle (زین)'; Persian often places the modifying noun after the head in poetry. برببست is the past tense 'bound/tied to' (بربست, poetic variant).
segments.s426-g4: بران باره = 'upon that/thereon the load' (بران = 'on/thereon', باره = 'load/burden'). پیل پیکر is a compound adjective-noun 'elephant-bodied' (پیل = elephant, پیکر = body/figure) describing the being who then نشست ('sat' / 'settled down', past). The verb نشست is simple past here.
segments.s426-g5: بفرمود (honorific/polite past) = 'he commanded/said'. تا شد = 'until it happened / until there came' (تا + شد marks the temporal/result clause). زواره is the proper name (the person/place brought), and برش here functions as a clitic phrase meaning 'to him / before him' (poetic pronominal attachment); so the whole chunk means 'he ordered until Zowara came before him / was brought to him.'
segments.s426-g6: فراوان (adverb) = 'abundantly/plentifully', سخن = 'speech/words'. راند is a past verb here meaning 'uttered / set forth' (in classical/poetic usage it can mean 'drove forth' and is used to mean 'uttered' in context). از introduces the topic 'about / concerning', and لشکرش = 'his army' (لشکر + pronominal suffix).
segments.s426-g7: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him'; گفت = 'said', past). رو لشکر آرای باش is an imperative instruction: لشکر آرای is a descriptive compound 'one who arrays/arranges the army' (لشکر + آرای), and باش is the 2nd‑person imperative 'be!' — so the phrase means 'Be the arranger of the army.' The particle رو here is emphatic/elliptical in poetry and helps the vocative/imperative sense.
segments.s426-g8: This is a location imperative: بر = 'on/upon', کوهه ریگ = 'the stony/sandy hill' (کوهه = hill/mountain, ریگ = sand/pebble), بر پای = 'at the foot/base' (بر + پای = 'on the foot of'), and باش = imperative 'be/stand'. Together: 'Stand on the sandy hill, at its foot.'
segments.s426-g49: نهاد is the past (3rd person singular) of نهادن 'to put/ place' (here: 'he put'). The object phrase آن بن نیزه را is marked by the direct-object particle را; آن (demonstrative 'that') modifies بن (here 'base/butt') which together with نیزه ('spear') forms 'the butt/base of that spear'. بر زمین is a prepositional phrase 'upon the ground' that shows the location of the putting.
segments.s426-g50: ز خاک سیاه is a source phrase 'from the black/dark earth' (ز = 'from', خاک = 'earth', سیاه = 'dark/black' follows the noun). اندر آمد is a classical-perhaps slightly archaic perfective 'came in/entered' (اندر = 'into/in'), and به زین marks the destination 'into/to the saddle' (به + زین). The clause expresses motion from the ground into the saddle.
segments.s426-g51: بسان is a simile marker 'like/as'. پلنگی here functions as a leopard-like noun/adjective 'a leopard' (classical Persian often uses adjective forms as nouns). که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; بر پشت گور is a locative phrase 'on the back of a grave' (بر = 'on', پشت = 'back', گور = 'grave').
segments.s426-g52: نشیند is a present/factual-form verb (3rd person singular) 'sits' used in the relative clause set by که; classical Persian often uses present-stem forms for general/frequent actions. برانگیزد is a present (3rd sing.) verb 'raises/rouses' (بر- prefix intensifies or indicates direction). از گور is the source 'from the grave' and شور is the thing produced 'passion/commotion/ardor' (object of برانگیزد). Word order places source and object after the verb in this poetic line.
segments.s426-g53: سپه is the subject 'the army/host'. در شگفتی is a prepositional phrase 'in astonishment' (در = 'in', شگفتی = 'astonishment/wonder'), and فروماندند is the past tense, 3rd person plural of فروماندن meaning 'they remained' or 'they were left/stayed' — here 'they remained [in astonishment]'.
segments.s426-g54: بران is an adverbial/relational particle 'thereupon/on that', نامدار is an epithet 'renowned/famed', and آفرین خواندند is a fixed collocation meaning 'they cried/bravo!' (آفرین = 'bravo/exclamation'; خواندند here is past plural 'they called/shouted'). The object of their shout is implicitly the famed man (نامدار).
segments.s426-g55: همی شد چو is a classical narrative formula 'it happened that when...' (همی marks ongoing or narrative aspect and شد = 'happened/it became'; چو = 'when'). نزد تهمتن رسید places the action 'he reached near Tahmatan' (نزد = 'near/in the presence of', تهمتن = proper name, رسید = past 'arrived/reached'). The clause sets the temporal background for the following observation.
segments.s426-g56: مر is an archaic/discourse particle (listed senses 'me'/'to me'); here it functions as an old dative/experiential particle and is not meant as a literal English 'me' — the clause means 'he saw him'. او را is the marked object 'him' (او + را), بران باره is a temporal/occasional phrase 'on that occasion' (بران = 'thereupon/thereon', باره = 'occasion'), تنها is 'alone', and بدید is the past 'he saw'. Together they state that, on that occasion, he saw the other man alone.
segments.s426-g89: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the clause. ایرانیان را is the definite direct object (ایرانیان + را). به + verbal noun + دادن (here کشتن + دادن) is an idiomatic causative: به کشتن دادن means 'to have put to death / to cause to be killed,' and دهم is the 1st-person form of دادن used here to express 'I cause / I put (to death)'. Word order places the object before the verbal expression, which is normal in classical Persian.
segments.s426-g90: خود is reflexive/emphatic 'myself / my own' (here marking that the crown will be on himself). اندر جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world' (اندر = classical 'in'). تاج بر سر نهم: تاج is the object 'crown', بر سر is the prepositional phrase 'upon (the) head', and نهم (from نهادن) is the 1st-person verb 'I put/place' — object-before-verb order and the use of نهم gives the sense 'I place the crown on (my) head.'
segments.s426-g91: منم is the emphatic copula 'I am.' پیشرو is the predicate 'the vanguard/forerunner.' The following clause هرک جنگ آیدم is a temporal/conditional subordinate clause: هرک (archaic هرگاه/هر که) 'whenever / whoever/whichever', جنگ 'battle', and آیدم 'I come' (1st-person). The verb comes at the end, as typical, so the sense is 'whenever battle comes, I come (forward).'
segments.s426-g92: وگر is the conditional 'and if / but if.' پیش here means 'before / in front of' (i.e., 'if before the battle'), جنگ 'battle', نهنگ is metaphorical here ('a huge one; mighty warrior' rather than literal whale), and آیدم 'I come.' The clause says 'even if a mighty (one) appears before the battle, I will come (forward).'
segments.s426-g93: ترا‌گر is a conditional address 'if you' (classical contraction of تو را گر / 'should you'). همی is an emphatic adverb ('indeed/just' here). یار is the object 'companion/ally.' باید is a modal 'it is necessary / you must,' and بیار is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'bring.' The clause thus addresses the addressee: 'If you indeed need a companion, you must bring (one).' Note classical word order and the use of باید with an imperative for force/necessity.
segments.s426-g94: م (here representing a cliticic element related to 'for me' / 'there is for me') plus را marks the beneficiary/emphatic object (roughly 'for me'). یار is the subject 'a companion.' هرگز negates ('never'), نیاید is the negative present/subjunctive form of آمدن 'to come' used idiomatically ('will/does not come / does not appear'), and به کار is part of the idiom به کار آمدن 'to be of use.' Together: 'A companion never comes to be of use to me' → 'A companion will never be useful for me.'
segments.s426-g95: مرا (here split as م + را) marks the possessor/beneficiary 'for me / I had.' یار is the predicate noun 'ally/companion.' در جنگ is the locative 'in battle,' یزدان is the subject 'God,' and بود is the past copula 'was.' Word order is the classical Persian 'For me a companion in battle was God' → 'God was my ally in battle.'
segments.s426-g96: سر و کار is an idiomatic compound meaning 'matters / affairs' (literally 'head and work'). با is the preposition 'with,' بخت خندان is a descriptive phrase 'smiling/favorable fortune' (i.e., good luck), and بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause means 'my affairs were with smiling fortune' → 'fortune smiled upon me / luck was on my side.'
segments.s426-g25: The clause begins with the explicit subject pronoun تو (you) followed by the time adverb اکنون (now). سپه را uses the object marker را after the direct object (سپه = 'the army'), and هم adds the meaning 'also/still' (here: 'also/at this place'). ایدر is an archaic locative meaning 'here'. بدار is an imperative form meaning 'hold/keep' (2nd person singular) — a conventional poetic command ‘you keep…’. Word order is subject — adverb — object — locative — verb (typical for elevated/poetic Persian).
segments.s426-g26: شوم is a 1st‑person subjunctive/optative (literally 'may I be' / 'I will be') introducing the speaker's intention to remain; تا here means 'until'. چه is the interrogative 'what', and پیش آورد is a verbal compound where پیش (literally 'before') combines with آورد ('bring about') to mean 'bring forth/produce'. روزگار (here 'fate') is the thing being brought about. The infinitival/subjunctive clause expresses duration/purpose: 'I will remain until what fate brings.'
segments.s426-g27: اگر marks a conditional 'if'. تند here functions as an adverb 'quickly/rapidly' modifying the verb. یابمش is the verb یاب 'find' with the enclitic object pronoun ـَش (him/it) attached, producing 'I find him/it'. هم in this position is emphatic ('even/still'), and زان is an archaic prepositional phrase 'from that' (i.e. 'by/from that'), linking to نشان ('sign'), which names the means/traces by which the speaker would find him.
segments.s426-g28: نخواهم is the negated first‑person present/future of خواستن used here as 'I will not (want/ask for)'. The short preposition ز (poetic 'from') introduces the source or people being refused: زابلستان names the region (Zabulistan). سرکشان is the final noun phrase; its form and meaning here denote persons from that land (contextually 'men/warriors/chiefs'), so the clause says the speaker will not call on or want those men from Zabulistan.
segments.s426-g29: به here functions instrumentally ('by/with'), introducing the means: 'with my lone body'. تنها (alone/only) modifies تن ('body, self'), and خوی + ش together form خویش ('his own'), where ـش is the possessive clitic 'his'. جویم is a 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive of 'seek' (I seek), and نبرد is the object 'battle/struggle'. The sequence places the instrumental phrase before the verb for emphasis: 'with only his body I seek battle.'
segments.s426-g30: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/on account of', here introducing the source/reason 'because of the army' (لشکر). نخواهم is again the negated 'I will not/would not want' (here 'I will not cause'), followed by کسی 'anyone/someone'. رنجه کرد is a light‑verb construction: رنجه (be distressed) + کرد (to cause/do) = 'to cause someone to suffer / to trouble someone'. The whole clause therefore says he refuses to put anyone to trouble on account of the army.
segments.s426-g31: کسی introduces an indefinite 'someone'. باشد here is optative/subjunctive ('may there be / let there be'), so the clause expresses a wish that such a person exist. از bakes the agent/source 'by/from fortune' (از بخت), and پیروز و شاد are predicates 'victorious and happy' describing that person.
segments.s426-g32: که introduces a relative clause 'who/whose'. باشد again is subjunctive/optative ('may be' / 'be'), always used in literary Persian for wished or potential states. همیشه 'always' modifies the clause; دلش is 'his heart' (دل + possessive ـش). پر ز literally 'full of' (پر = full, ز = 'of/from') governs داد 'justice', so the phrase means 'whose heart is always full of justice.'

sentences.s426-s61: They were scattered from the battlefield; the horses were stricken with grief and the men lay ruined.
sentences.s426-s62: Their mouths foamed with blood and dust; all the gabrs and the (arm-)garments were rent to pieces.
sentences.s426-s21: He commanded that they fetch the cuirass and his own — that quiver and the warrior's spear.
sentences.s426-s22: They brought it, and he put on the cuirass; they set that Kayanian helmet upon his head.
sentences.s426-s23: He ordered that they place the saddle on the black steed and bring it near the king.
sentences.s426-s24: When he put on the cuirass, he became aggressive because of the strength and the joy that were within him.
sentences.s426-s29: After a while he spoke with Peshotan, saying that we must not be his companion and mate.
sentences.s426-s30: If he is alone, we too should be alone; from that low place we will swiftly rise up.
sentences.s426-s31: In that manner both of them went to battle; you said that there is no revelry in the world.
sentences.s426-s32: When the old man and the young man drew near, two proud lions and two champions (stood ready).
sentences.s426-s37: Tell me, so that I may bring a rider from Zabul who will be armed with a Kabul dagger.
sentences.s426-s38: I will bring them to that battlefield to fight; I myself will here pause for a while.
sentences.s426-s39: May the shedding of blood be to your liking; may you see Tegapuy and hanging.
sentences.s426-s40: Esfandiyar replied thus: 'What do you say so often, you wicked one?'
sentences.s426-s17: It passed the river's edge and rose aloft; the world stood amazed at the wondrous deed.
sentences.s426-s18: A roar rang out: 'O fortunate Esfandiyar — your rival has come to marshal for the deed.'
sentences.s426-s19: When Esfandiyar heard these words from that ancient, quarrelsome lion,
sentences.s426-s20: He laughed and said, 'Now I am dressed/ready; at that moment I rose from sleep.'
sentences.s426-s41: You rose from the iwan at nightfall and from that swift height you sought me.
sentences.s426-s42: Why did you devise bonds and guile and deceit? Truly you saw the narrow descent.
